output:
# Warp-Confounded, Blur-Rewarding Consistency: Promoting 3D Style-Transfer's Single Warped-LPIPS Scalar to a Warp-Marginalized, Blur-Controlled Profile
*Reframing multi-view consistency from one confounded number into a measurement with identifiable error sources - method-attributable consistency, warp-estimator error, and their interaction as a separate third component - that provably collapses back to the published scalar.*

## Research Analysis

### 1. Background and current state of the art

Neural radiance fields (NeRF; Mildenhall et al. 2020) represent a scene as a continuous volumetric function optimized from posed images, and have become the default substrate for 3D reconstruction and editing. Training was accelerated by Instant-NGP multiresolution hash encodings (Mueller et al. 2022) and extended to unbounded captures by Mip-NeRF 360 (Barron et al. 2022). The Nerfstudio framework and its nerfacto default (Tancik et al. 2023) consolidate these advances into the reproducible pipeline that most editing and stylization papers now build on, which means a single evaluation convention adopted there propagates across the whole sub-field. Editing a radiance field is, almost by definition, a multi-view problem: a stylized scene is only useful if it looks like the same scene from every camera, so "view consistency" has become the headline reliability claim that 3D style-transfer methods compete on.

Stylizing or editing a radiance field is dominated by two families. The first is diffusion-guided instruction editing: Instruct-NeRF2NeRF (Haque et al. 2023) iteratively re-renders dataset views, edits each with InstructPix2Pix (Brooks et al. 2023), and re-fits the field; ViCA-NeRF (Dong and Wang 2023) adds explicit view-consistency handling via depth and blending. The second is feature / optimization style transfer: ARF (Zhang et al. 2022) introduces a nearest-neighbor feature matching (NNFM) loss; StyleRF (Liu et al. 2023) performs zero-shot feature-space stylization; Ref-NPR (Zhang et al. 2023) does reference-based controllable stylization; SNeRF (Nguyen-Phuoc et al. 2022) alternates stylization and NeRF fitting. The source method that motivates this analysis combines style-aligned diffusion (StyleAligned; Hertz et al. 2024), a depth ControlNet (Zhang et al. 2023), and a sliced-Wasserstein (SWD) style loss (Heitz et al. 2021) to claim "perceptually view-consistent" 3D style transfer. That pipeline is doubly exposed: the SWD loss matches feature distributions rather than spatial detail, and re-fitting a field to a stack of independently previewed diffusion edits tends to average out high-frequency disagreement, so the very mechanism that buys consistency can also buy smoothing - precisely the degeneracy a consistency metric must not reward. The final radiance field also need not match the previewed diffusion images, yet no preview-fidelity number is reported. Across both families the load-bearing claim is the same word - consistent - and across both families it is certified the same way, which is what makes a single shared metric flaw a shared liability.

How is "view consistency" scored today? Almost universally by a single warped-LPIPS scalar: render two views, align one to the other with a warp estimator - RAFT optical flow (Teed and Deng 2020) or depth-reprojection from a MiDaS-class depth model - then measure LPIPS (Zhang et al. 2018) over the valid overlap, at both short range and long range; a CLIP score (Radford et al. 2021) supplies a complementary style / text signal. Crucially, the warp estimator is fixed and unmarginalized, occlusion masking is ad hoc, the LPIPS backbone is chosen by convention, and there is no standard 3D-style-transfer consistency benchmark. The occlusion mask is itself a hidden knob: it decides which pixels count toward the score, so a loose threshold can quietly exclude the hard disoccluded regions where inconsistency actually shows up, and a different flow net or depth model produces a different mask and therefore a different "consistency" reading of the same field. Every method named above reports this scalar, and none decomposes it. Meanwhile, a mature statistical lineage targets exactly this kind of measurement problem: generalizability theory (Cronbach et al. 1972) and linear mixed / variance-components models (lme4; Bates et al. 2015) separate a signal of interest from the nuisance facets that contaminate it, and Bouthillier et al. (2021) imported this thinking into ML benchmarking. That lineage has never been applied to 3D-style-transfer consistency. The unoccupied gap it leaves is precisely what we name **Warp-Confounded, Blur-Rewarding Consistency (WBC)**: a single warped-LPIPS scalar, computed under one fixed warp estimator, that conflates a method's genuine radiance-field consistency with the warp estimator's own error, and that is actively minimized by a degenerate over-smoothed field with nothing left to be inconsistent about.

### 2. Key findings and analysis

The central finding is that the field's "view-consistent" certification rests on a single number carrying two independent, uncontrolled failure modes at once. First, warped-LPIPS is a *confounded* measurement: aligning frame i to frame j requires a warp estimator, and that estimator - whether RAFT-class optical flow or depth-reprojection - has its own error. The resulting score therefore sums method-attributable 3D inconsistency and warp-attributable misalignment, and no amount of staring at one scalar can tell them apart. A method can look more "consistent" simply because a particular flow net or depth model happened to align its frames more forgivingly. Second, warped-LPIPS has a *trivial minimizer*: a sufficiently blurred radiance field has almost no high-frequency content to disagree on across views, so it scores near-perfect consistency by destroying exactly the detail that made the edit worth doing. These two pathologies - warp confounding and blur reward - are not exotic corner cases; they are structural properties of the metric every method in Section 1 reports, which is why WBC is a field-wide diagnosis rather than a complaint about one paper.

WBC makes the failure quantifiable by modeling per-(method, scene, view-pair, warp-estimator) warped-LPIPS with a crossed random-effects variance-components model (REML, lme4 lineage). Method is a fixed effect; warp-estimator, scene, and view-pair are crossed random effects; and the METHOD x WARP-ESTIMATOR INTERACTION is carried as a *separate, explicitly labeled third variance component*, never folded into a main effect. Each fitted variance component is read as a share of total warped-LPIPS variation - an intraclass-style breakdown that says, in plain terms, how much of what we have been calling "consistency" is the method, how much is the warp estimator, how much is the scene, and how much is the irreducible method-by-warp interaction. The expected decomposition is the crux. Under a single fixed warp (current practice) the warp contribution is invisible by construction, so all variation is misattributed to method. Once the warp-estimator ensemble is introduced, we expect the warp-estimator main effect and - critically - the method x warp interaction to claim a non-negligible share of total variance: large enough that method rankings computed under one warp can flip under another, which is the operational signature of confounding. We expect stratified bootstrap CIs over scenes and styles to be wide enough that several methods' rank intervals overlap, meaning the naive single-warp leaderboard is not rank-stable and its apparent ordering is partly an artifact of the chosen estimator. The interaction term matters most precisely because it cannot be removed by averaging: a method that is consistent under flow but not under depth-reprojection (or vice versa) lives entirely in that interaction, and a naive multi-warp average would silently collapse it into a main effect and hide it. Separately, the blurred-field placebo is expected to rank deceptively *high* on raw warped-LPIPS - near the top - yet rank correctly *low* once consistency is reported net of sharpness, while the two-seed relabeled placebo should show approximately zero method-attributable difference; together these two legs demonstrate that the corrected profile catches a degeneracy the published scalar rewards and does not invent differences where none exist.

It is essential to separate reducible from structural error. The reducible sources are the warp estimator (flow-net choice, depth model, occlusion-mask threshold), the perceptual readout (LPIPS backbone, AlexNet vs VGG), and view-pair sampling; these are nuisance facets that a marginalization ensemble can average over and whose contribution the variance-components model can size and report. The structural source is different in kind: there is no ground truth for the "true 3D consistency" of a *stylized* field, because no clean stylized reference exists to compare against. WBC does not pretend to manufacture one. Instead it pins the measurement operationally from three sides - the singleton-collapse anchor, multi-warp agreement as the warp-invariant (method-attributable) component, and two placebo legs that bound tool validity from both directions. The anchor is exact: under a singleton warp estimator {W0}, uniform view-pair weights, and the blur coefficient fixed to zero, the method-attributable term equals the standard warped-LPIPS scalar (as recomputed under that single warp) exactly by construction, and this identity is unit-testable to machine precision. That B1 collapse is what makes the whole profile a strict, backward-compatible generalization of current practice rather than a competing metric: it contradicts no published number and adds the missing axes on top.

### 3. Novel insights or recommendations

The core recommendation is to stop reporting 3D-style-transfer consistency as a single warped-LPIPS scalar and instead adopt the warp-estimator-marginalized, blur-controlled consistency *profile* as the reporting standard, paired with the style / content / consistency Pareto front as the way trade-offs are communicated. Concretely, novelty is positioned along five pillars, each naming what it improves on. (i) Consistency as a *confounded measurement*: WBC is the first to decompose multi-view consistency into method-attributable consistency, warp-estimator-attributable error, and their interaction as a separately labeled third component, where Instruct-NeRF2NeRF and ViCA-NeRF report warped-LPIPS under a single warp and never separate these sources. (ii) Blur-reward as a *first-class, tested axis*: a degenerate-blurred-field placebo that any honest metric must rank low, plus a sharpness covariate reporting consistency net of blur - a check ARF, StyleRF, and Ref-NPR never run, so their metrics silently reward smoothing. (iii) Estimator variation as a *geometry-style entanglement probe*: swapping the depth model inside the depth-reprojection warp measures how much "consistency" is really depth-conditioning, invisible to any single fixed warp. (iv) The style x content x consistency *trilemma as a Pareto profile* instead of a cherry-picked scalar column. (v) The exact B1 collapse, a recovery guarantee no prior consistency metric offers.

Two of these pillars are the keystones for adoption. The blurred-field placebo should become a *mandatory sanity check* - a metric that cannot rank a deliberately smoothed field below a genuinely consistent one is not measuring consistency, and shipping the placebo alongside any consistency claim turns this from an argument into a unit test. The B1 collapse is the frictionless-adoption keystone: because the profile reduces exactly to the published scalar at its base rung, a method's existing reported number is simply the B1 reading of its full profile, so authors lose nothing by migrating and gain the warp and blur axes for free. The same pathologies recur beyond NeRF. In 3D Gaussian Splatting stylization the substrate changes but the certification does not - consistency is still read through a warped perceptual distance, so the warp confound and the blur reward survive intact, and an over-smoothed splat field games the metric exactly as an over-smoothed radiance field does. In video and 4D editing the "warp" is temporal optical flow rather than a multi-view reprojection, but it is the same confounded alignment step, and temporal stability scored this way carries the same two failure modes. Any generative-3D system that certifies temporal or multi-view stability with a warped perceptual distance therefore inherits both pathologies, and the crossed variance-components recipe ports directly: relabel the random facet (flow net, depth model, or temporal estimator), keep the interaction term explicit, keep the sharpness control first-class, and the same profile applies. WBC is thus a template for an entire class of stability metrics, not a one-scene fix. That said, the empirical conclusions here are established on the tested scene sets - Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, LLFF, and the Instruct-NeRF2NeRF scenes - and extension to 3DGS, video, and 4D domains is a hypothesis to validate rather than a demonstrated result.

A responsible-evaluation note is built into the design rather than bolted on. The purpose of marginalizing the warp is *fair method comparison*: methods should be ranked on method-attributable consistency, not on whichever flow net or depth model happened to flatter them, so that adoption, citation, and follow-up build on real differences. The purpose of the blur control is to refuse to certify content-destroying blur as "consistent": a method that wins the consistency axis by erasing the high-frequency content the user asked to stylize has not succeeded, and the Pareto front - with blur linking the consistency and content axes - makes that failure legible instead of letting it hide behind a single best column. WBC reframes consistency from a leaderboard entry into a measurement with identifiable error sources, which is the high-leverage move for a field standardizing its evaluation right now: it changes what counts as progress. Because the style-transfer methods under evaluation are generative editing tools that could also be applied to synthetic-media creation, transparent and degeneracy-aware evaluation additionally supports responsible reporting.

### 4. References and evidence

1. Mildenhall, Srinivasan, Tancik, Barron, Ramamoorthi, Ng (2020). "NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis." ECCV 2020.
2. Tancik, Weber, Ng, Li, Yi, Kerr, Wang, Kristoffersen, Austin, Salahi, Ahuja, McAllister, Kanazawa (2023). "Nerfstudio: A Modular Framework for Neural Radiance Field Development." ACM SIGGRAPH 2023.
3. Haque, Tancik, Efros, Holynski, Kanazawa (2023). "Instruct-NeRF2NeRF: Editing 3D Scenes with Instructions." ICCV 2023.
4. Brooks, Holynski, Efros (2023). "InstructPix2Pix: Learning to Follow Image Editing Instructions." CVPR 2023.
5. Dong, Wang (2023). "ViCA-NeRF: View-Consistency-Aware 3D Editing of Neural Radiance Fields." NeurIPS 2023.
6. Liu, Zhan, Chen, Zhang, Yu, El Saddik, Lu, Xing (2023). "StyleRF: Zero-Shot 3D Style Transfer of Neural Radiance Fields." CVPR 2023.
7. Zhang, Kolkin, Bi, Luan, Xu, Shechtman, Snavely (2022). "ARF: Artistic Radiance Fields." ECCV 2022.
8. Zhang, He, Xing, Yao, Jia (2023). "Ref-NPR: Reference-Based Non-Photorealistic Radiance Fields for Controllable Scene Stylization." CVPR 2023.
9. Hertz, Voynov, Fruchter, Cohen-Or (2024). "Style Aligned Image Generation via Shared Attention." CVPR 2024.
10. Zhang, Rao, Agrawala (2023). "Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models." (ControlNet) ICCV 2023.
11. Heitz, Vanhoey, Chambon, Belcour (2021). "A Sliced Wasserstein Loss for Neural Texture Synthesis." CVPR 2021.
12. Zhang, Isola, Efros, Shechtman, Wang (2018). "The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Deep Features as a Perceptual Metric." (LPIPS) CVPR 2018.
13. Teed, Deng (2020). "RAFT: Recurrent All-Pairs Field Transforms for Optical Flow." ECCV 2020.
14. Bates, Maechler, Bolker, Walker (2015). "Fitting Linear Mixed-Effects Models Using lme4." Journal of Statistical Software, 67(1).
15. Bouthillier, Delaunay, Bronzi, Trofimov, Nichyporuk, Szeto, Mohammadi Sepahvand, Raff, Madan, Voleti, Enger Kahou, Michalski, Arbel, Pal, Varoquaux, Vincent (2021). "Accounting for Variance in Machine Learning Benchmarks." MLSys (Proceedings of Machine Learning and Systems) 2021.
16. Cronbach, Gleser, Nanda, Rajaratnam (1972). "The Dependability of Behavioral Measurements: Theory of Generalizability for Scores and Profiles." Wiley. (Generalizability theory)

Secondary works cited inline for background grounding: Instant-NGP (Mueller et al. 2022); Mip-NeRF 360 (Barron et al. 2022); SNeRF (Nguyen-Phuoc et al. 2022); CLIP (Radford et al. 2021); GMFlow (Xu et al. 2022), SEA-RAFT (Wang et al. 2024), and MiDaS-class depth (Ranftl et al. 2020-2022) as warp-estimator ensemble members.

## Proposed Research Idea (5q)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can scoring a 3D NeRF style-transfer method's multi-view consistency with a single warped-LPIPS scalar computed under one fixed warp estimator conflate the radiance field's genuine 3D consistency with the warp estimator's own optical-flow or depth-reprojection error while also rewarding a degenerate over-smoothed field that scores consistent merely by being blurred, so that the scalar must be promoted to a warp-estimator-marginalized, blur-controlled consistency profile that separates method-attributable consistency from warp-attributable error, reports their interaction as a third labeled component, and provably collapses back to the standard warped-LPIPS scalar under a singleton warp estimator with uniform view-pair weights and the blur control disabled?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

3D editing and generative-3D are moving fast, yet their headline reliability claim - "perceptually view-consistent" - rests on one confounded number. If that number conflates a method's genuine radiance-field consistency with the warp estimator's own flow or reprojection error, then the entire comparative literature (Instruct-NeRF2NeRF, ViCA-NeRF, StyleRF, ARF, Ref-NPR) is ranking methods partly on a nuisance nobody controls. Worse, because warped-LPIPS is minimized by an over-smoothed field, the metric can hand a "win" to a method that merely destroyed high-frequency content. These are not abstract concerns: such scores decide which methods get adopted, cited, and built upon, so an error in the metric compounds into an error in the field's sense of direction.

WBC matters for four concrete reasons. (1) Fair method comparison: a warp-estimator-marginalized, blur-corrected profile compares methods on method-attributable consistency, not on whichever warp estimator happened to flatter them. (2) Degeneracy detection: the blur control plus the degenerate-blurred-field placebo expose "consistency" purchased by smoothing, guarding against a Goodhart collapse in which everyone quietly optimizes blur because the metric pays for it. (3) Standardization for an emerging area: with no agreed 3D-style-transfer consistency benchmark, a backward-compatible profile that exactly recovers the published scalar (the B1 collapse) offers a migration path that contradicts no prior number while adding the missing axes, so adoption costs almost nothing. (4) Transfer: the same warp-confound and blur-reward pathologies recur in 3D Gaussian Splatting stylization, video / 4D editing, and any generative-3D system that certifies temporal or multi-view stability with warped perceptual distances, and the variance-components recipe ports directly.

The broader implication is methodological. WBC reframes "consistency" from a scalar leaderboard entry into a measurement with identifiable error sources, and renders the style / content / consistency trilemma as a Pareto profile so a method's trade-offs are legible rather than hidden behind a cherry-picked best column. For a field standardizing its evaluation right now, getting the consistency metric right is high-leverage: it changes what counts as progress, and it does so for every downstream system that will inherit today's evaluation conventions through shared pipelines like Nerfstudio.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The hard parts are real, not cosmetic, and each one defeats an obvious shortcut. (i) The warped-LPIPS confound is invisible to single-warp practice: with one fixed estimator you simply cannot tell method-attributable 3D consistency from the warp's own flow or depth-reprojection error, because the two are summed into one scalar. The tempting naive fix - averaging warped-LPIPS across several warps - still hides the structure, because it collapses the METHOD x WARP-ESTIMATOR interaction into a main effect and discards exactly the information that reveals confounding. Only a crossed random-effects model with an explicit, separately labeled interaction term recovers it; a method that is consistent under optical flow but not under depth-reprojection lives entirely in that interaction, and any averaging erases it.

(ii) The blur-reward degeneracy means the raw metric has a trivial minimizer: a sufficiently blurred field has nothing left to be inconsistent about, so it scores near-perfect. Adding more warp estimators does not fix this, because every estimator rewards the same smoothing - the degeneracy is in the content, not the alignment. Disentangling sharpness from consistency therefore requires the sharpness residualization plus the blurred-field placebo as a live catch, not just a bigger ensemble.

(iii) There is no ground truth for the "true 3D consistency" of a stylized field, because no clean stylized reference exists to measure against. This is the structural obstacle, and it is why a purely data-driven "just measure the error" framing fails: there is no error to measure against. We address it operationally rather than pretending otherwise: the singleton-collapse anchor (B1) ties the profile to the published scalar; multi-warp agreement defines method-attributable consistency as the warp-invariant component, on the principle that what survives across independent estimators is the method's own behavior rather than any one estimator's quirk; and the two placebo legs - a relabeled-seed null and a blurred-field degeneracy null - bound tool validity from both sides at once. The relabeled-seed leg checks that the tool does not invent differences between identical methods; the blurred-field leg checks that the tool does catch the degeneracy it must catch. A tool that passes both is trustworthy in exactly the regime we use it.

(iv) The effective sample is small (few scenes), which inflates variance-component confidence intervals and threatens rank stability; variance-components estimates are notoriously data-hungry, and a handful of scenes is not many. The mitigation is a stratified bootstrap plus many short- and long-range view-pairs per scene, which raises effective N without requiring new captures, while stratifying by scene and style so the bootstrap respects the dependence structure rather than treating correlated view-pairs as independent draws. (v) Re-stylizing many scenes times methods is compute-heavy. The mitigation is decisive for feasibility: reuse public stylized outputs and released checkpoints, and note that the re-scoring itself needs no NeRF retraining - it is a pure post-hoc analysis over already-rendered frames - which is what makes the full B1-to-B4 ladder runnable within realistic resources. The expensive part (training and stylizing fields) is done once or reused; the contribution (the marginalized, blur-controlled decomposition) is cheap to compute and cheap for others to re-run on their own outputs.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work has not solved it because every 3D-style-transfer paper treats multi-view consistency as a fixed scalar to be optimized and reported, never as a measurement to be decomposed - so the tools to separate signal from nuisance were simply never brought to bear. There is also a structural incentive: the same scalar is both the thing a method optimizes and the thing it is graded on, so questioning the grader is questioning one's own headline result, and a single number is far easier to put in a results table than a profile. Positioned against the specific methods: Instruct-NeRF2NeRF (Haque et al. 2023) reports warped-LPIPS and CLIP under a single warp and never separates method-attributable consistency from warp error. ViCA-NeRF (Dong and Wang 2023) is the sharpest illustration: it puts "view consistency" in its very name and adds depth-and-blending machinery to improve it, yet still certifies the result with a single-warp scalar that leaves the claimed consistency entangled with the warp estimator's own error - the metric cannot confirm that the named feature works for the reason claimed, and because its depth handling is exactly the kind of geometry conditioning a depth-reprojection warp also uses, the score risks crediting the method for agreement that the warp itself manufactured. StyleRF (Liu et al. 2023), ARF (Zhang et al. 2022, via NNFM), and Ref-NPR (Zhang et al. 2023) each optimize and report a consistency scalar but never test whether a blurred field games it, so a failure mode their metrics silently reward goes unmeasured; ARF's NNFM loss in particular can smooth toward matched features, the precise direction the blur control is built to flag. The source SWD-based method inherits all of this: its "perceptually view-consistent" claim cannot be distinguished from warp-estimator tolerance or from SWD-induced smoothing, and it provides no preview-fidelity quantification, so there is no check that the final field even matches the diffusion previews it was fit to.

The deeper barrier is that the right correction lives in a different literature. The marginalization machinery WBC needs - crossed random-effects variance-components models (lme4; Bates et al. 2015) and generalizability theory (Cronbach et al. 1972), brought into ML benchmarking by Bouthillier et al. (2021) - was never connected to 3D-style-transfer consistency. WBC differs and improves on prior work in five specific ways that map onto that machinery: it models consistency as a decomposition with the method x warp-estimator interaction as a separate labeled component (vs a fixed scalar); it makes blur-reward a first-class, tested axis with a placebo any honest metric must rank low (vs an untested silent reward); it varies the depth model inside the warp to probe geometry-style entanglement (vs a single fixed warp that hides it); it reports the style / content / consistency trilemma as a Pareto front (vs a cherry-picked column); and it provides an exact B1 collapse to the published scalar (vs no recovery guarantee at all). No prior consistency metric offers that recovery guarantee, which is precisely why the profile can be a strict, backward-compatible superset rather than a rival.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Methods and scenes. We stylize a fixed scene set with several methods so the profile has a real ranking target: the source SWD-NeRF method, Instruct-NeRF2NeRF (Nerfstudio implementation), ViCA-NeRF, StyleRF, and ARF (NNFM). Three deliberate placebos are inserted as methods: (a) a Gaussian / low-pass-filtered radiance field, (b) an over-smoothed high-TV-regularized field, and (c) two seeds of one real method relabeled as two distinct "methods" (the tool-validity null). Scenes are real captures with novel-view trajectories - Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, LLFF, and the Instruct-NeRF2NeRF scenes (bear / face / farm), plus additional Nerfstudio captures - all trained with nerfacto in Nerfstudio, including large outdoor 360 captures so the evaluation is not synthetic-only. Along each trajectory we render both short-range and long-range view-pairs.

Warp-estimator ensemble (the marginalization axis). Optical flow: RAFT, GMFlow, SEA-RAFT. Depth-reprojection warps using several depth sources: the NeRF's own rendered depth; monocular MiDaS / DPT / Depth-Anything; and COLMAP MVS depth. We vary occlusion-mask thresholds and vary the LPIPS backbone (AlexNet vs VGG) as a secondary readout factor. Each estimator independently aligns frame i to frame j, and warped-LPIPS is scored on the valid overlap. Varying the depth model inside the warp is what directly probes depth-conditioning / geometry-style entanglement.

Decomposition. We model per-(method, scene, view-pair, warp-estimator) warped-LPIPS with a crossed random-effects / linear mixed model (REML, lme4-style). Method is a fixed effect; warp-estimator, scene, and view-pair are crossed random effects; and the METHOD x WARP-ESTIMATOR INTERACTION is a separate labeled variance component, never folded into a main effect. We report variance shares (percent) for method, warp, scene, and the interaction; a stratified bootstrap over view-pairs and scenes for rank-stability confidence intervals; and a pairwise significance map across methods. The fixed-effect method contrasts give the warp-marginalized ranking, the random-effect variance shares size each nuisance facet, and the bootstrap resamples scene and style strata so the reported rank intervals reflect the small-scene regime honestly rather than pretending to more precision than a handful of captures can support. Short-range and long-range view-pairs enter as distinct levels so that drift accumulating over a long trajectory is not averaged away by abundant easy short-baseline pairs.

Blur control. A first-class blur / sharpness axis - per-field high-frequency energy / gradient-magnitude, computed no-reference - is added, and the model residualizes consistency on sharpness so a method cannot buy consistency with smoothing. Validation is explicit: the blurred-field placebo must score near-perfect raw warped-LPIPS yet rank low on the corrected profile.

Trilemma Pareto. We jointly report style fidelity (CLIP-style / Gram / ArtFID-style), content preservation (depth / edge / structure retention vs the source), and corrected consistency, and plot methods as points on a Pareto front rather than a single ranking. Blur links the content and consistency axes, exposing any method that "wins" consistency by destroying content.

The B1-to-B4 ladder. B1 = singleton warp {W0} + uniform view-pair weights + blur coefficient fixed to zero, under which the method-attributable term equals the published warped-LPIPS scalar exactly by construction, asserted via a unit test to machine precision (if we later re-implement any published number, that comparison is reported "within reproduction tolerance," never "exact"). B2 adds the flow-net and depth-reprojection ensemble, making warp variance and the interaction visible. B3 adds the blur control, catching the degeneracy and reporting consistency net of blur. B4 adds the trilemma Pareto plus cross-style / scene marginalization. Each rung states its added insight: B1 the backward-compatible anchor, B2 the size of the warp confound, B3 the blur degeneracy, B4 the operating-point trade-off.

Metrics summary and expected outcomes. We report (1) variance-share percent per component including the interaction; (2) bootstrap rank-CI width; (3) placebo-rank (the blurred field must be low); (4) warp-marginalized method-attributable consistency; and (5) Pareto dominance count. We expect the warp-estimator and method x warp interaction components to claim a non-trivial share of variance, single-warp rankings to be bootstrap-unstable, and the blurred-field placebo to invert from top on raw warped-LPIPS to bottom on the corrected profile, while the two-seed relabeled placebo shows approximately zero method-attributable difference. We further expect at least one method's single-warp rank to flip when the warp estimator is swapped (the direct, visible signature of the interaction), and the depth-model swap inside the depth-reprojection warp to move scores measurably for methods that lean on depth conditioning - quantifying geometry-style entanglement that a single fixed warp cannot see. Ablations isolate each factor: drop the interaction term and show variance is misattributed to method; disable the blur residualization and show the placebo climbs back to the top; collapse the flow and depth warps to a singleton and recover B1. Because the corrected profile reduces exactly to the single published scalar at B1, WBC is a strict, backward-compatible generalization of current practice - it contradicts no prior result and adds the warp, interaction, and blur axes the field has been missing.
